VM OS mix skewed heavily to Windows (95.2% Windows vs 4.8% Linux): because the compute/virtualmachines ostype aggregation shows Windows dominates the current estate, your Terraform plan must explicitly select Linux images and supply SSH key provisioning and Linux-specific extensions/config to ensure the new tier VMs are deployed and managed correctly.

Managed identity usage is pervasive on VMs (98.8% SystemAssigned and UserAssigned): because almost all VMs already have both system- and user-assigned identities, plan to provision or reuse user-assigned managed identities in Terraform for VM access, Automation runbooks and Log Analytics ingestion to match tenant conventions and simplify RBAC assignments.

VM onboarding via extensions is common (3,639 VM extensions with recurring registration/configuration settings): because extension-based registration appears widely used, design your Terraform deployment to install and configure the same VM extensions (for management, monitoring and registration) as part of Linux VM provisioning so patching and logging can be automated consistently.

Azure Automation footprint is small (3 Automation Accounts, Basic SKU, public network access True and ~10 runbooks): because existing automation capacity is limited and uses Basic SKU with publicnetworkaccess enabled, plan to either add Automation Accounts (regionally or centrally) and runbooks or centralize Update Management linkage to a well-sized Automation Account and Log Analytics workspace to handle tenant-wide patch orchestration.

Network flow capture is currently rare (only 4 networkwatchers/flowlogs) while NSGs are abundant (957 NSGs) and VNets number 205: because there is a large surface area of NSGs but few flow logs, include in your plan enabling NSG flow logs (or deploying vNet TAP where packet capture is required) across spoke/hub NSGs and centralize flows into Log Analytics or a secure storage account for the compliance traffic-logging requirement.

Log Analytics and DCRs are well represented (149 workspaces and 57 datacollectionrules with common destinations like 'ciworkspace'): because tenant conventions already route telemetry to named Log Analytics workspaces and DCRs, plan to reuse or extend those existing workspaces/DCRs for Update Management and NSG flow log ingestion rather than creating isolated workspaces.

Storage accounts suitable for sinks are prevalent and mostly secure (443 storage accounts with TLS1_2 enforced by 96.6% and mixed publicnetworkaccess): because storage accounts are available and typically enforce TLS1_2 but have varied network access settings, select storage accounts with TLS1_2 and appropriate network ACLs for flow log or diagnostic sinks and align Terraform to the tenant's publicnetworkaccess and network ACL patterns.

Subnet and naming patterns exist (205 VNets with common subnet names like 'default' and 'appSubnet' and 205 virtualnetworkpeerings): because subnets and peering naming conventions are observable, define tier-specific subnets (web/app/db) and NSG-per-subnet in Terraform using consistent naming and peering patterns to integrate cleanly with the existing virtual network topology and policy expectations.
